text: The prepared left colon and sigmoid colon are visible in the surgical field during a low anterior resection for rectal cancer. The mesocolon has been divided at the rectosigmoid junction and at a proximal point on the descending colon, selected for colonic transection.

Indocyanine green dye is administered, and white light laparoscopy shows satisfactory perfusion at the proximal colonic transection point. The dye uptake is visible in the small bowel, but there is an absence of fluorescence in the descending colon up to a point approximately ten centimeters more proximal than the selected transection site.

The surgeon observes that the initially selected segment of colon for transection lacks perfusion under white light. The colonic mesocolon is re-prepared at a more proximal point, and the stapler is repositioned to secure transection within a viable zone. Excellent perfusion is now visible in the newly selected colonic segment.
The surgeon observes satisfactory perfusion within the rectal remnant and performs distal transection at the rectosigmoid junction.

The surgical specimen is removed, and an anvil is placed within the proximal colonic segment outside the body. The circular stapler is inserted transanally and advanced through the rectal stump. The anvil is then aligned with the stapler head to perform the colorectal anastomosis.

Indocyanine green is administered to assess perfusion of the in situ colorectal anastomosis. The near-infrared view confirms satisfactory fluorescence in both the colonic and rectal segments, indicating adequate perfusion.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis to visually confirm the mechanical integrity of the connection between the colonic and rectal segments.
